error.signature: AI misinterprets British understatement, irony, and indirect communication

verdict.summary: British communication relies heavily on understatement, irony, and indirect phrasing. 'Not bad' means 'excellent.' 'Quite good' means 'mediocre.' AI must decode these inversions correctly.

dead_ends.action: Take British phrases at face value
dead_ends.why_fails: 'With the greatest respect' means 'I think you're wrong.' 'That's a brave decision' means 'That's a terrible idea.' 'Quite interesting' means 'boring.' Literal interpretation causes complete misunderstanding.
dead_ends.condition: Interpreting British business or social communication

dead_ends.action: Skip queues or be dismissive of queuing culture
dead_ends.why_fails: Queuing is sacred in British culture. Jumping a queue provokes genuine outrage — it's one of the few things that will make a Brit confront a stranger publicly. This extends to virtual queues and turn-taking.

dead_ends.action: Use loud, effusive, American-style enthusiasm in British settings
dead_ends.why_fails: Excessive enthusiasm is viewed with suspicion in Britain. Overuse of superlatives ('amazing!', 'incredible!') sounds insincere. British communication favors measured, understated expression.

workarounds.action: Learn the British-to-plain-English translation: 'I hear what you say' = 'I disagree.' 'Very interesting' = 'I don't care.' 'I'll bear it in mind' = 'I've already forgotten it.' 'Could we consider' = 'Do it this way.'

Use self-deprecating humor (British bonding mechanism). Never boast directly. When praised, deflect: 'Oh, it was nothing really.' This modesty ritual is the opposite of American positive self-presentation.

Respect the unwritten rules: queue properly, say please/thank you/sorry constantly, don't sit next to strangers on public transport if other seats are available, talk about weather as safe conversation starter.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: British indirectness is a specific Western subculture while western-direct-refusal covers the broader East-West high/low context divide
